content:
# Revelation Chapter 13 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Book:** Revelation | **Chapter:** 13

**13:1 -- "I stood" and opening phrasing:**
- EMET: "And I stood upon the sand of the sea. And I saw a beast coming up out of the sea..."
- LOGOS: "And I saw a beast rising out of the sea..." (omits the "I stood" clause)
- PNEUMA: "Then I took my stand on the sand at the edge of the sea. And I watched as a beast came rising up out of the ocean..."
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA in retaining the "I stood" clause, as Sinaiticus reads estathen (first person). LOGOS omitted it without note. The verb "rising" (LOGOS) is preferred over "coming up" (EMET) for English fluency. "Sea" is retained over PNEUMA's "ocean."

**13:1 -- "names of blasphemy" distribution:**
- EMET: "upon the heads of it names of blasphemy"
- LOGOS: "upon his heads names of blasphemy"
- PNEUMA: "on every head a blasphemous title"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in preserving the plural "names of blasphemy" directly, as this matches Sinaiticus onomata. PNEUMA's singular "title" distributed over "every head" departs from the form.

**13:2 -- Pronoun for the beast:**
- EMET: "it" throughout, with "the feet of it," "the mouth of it"
- LOGOS: "his" throughout -- "his feet," "his mouth"
- PNEUMA: "its feet were massive like a bear's and its mouth gaped wide"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "its" for the beast, following PNEUMA's neuter pronoun. LOGOS's masculine "his" reflects Greek grammatical gender but over-personalizes in English. EMET's construction is too stilted.

**13:3 -- Description of the healed wound:**
- EMET: "one of the heads of it as having been slain to death, and the wound of the death of it was healed"
- LOGOS: "one of his heads was as though slain unto death, and his wound of death was healed"
- PNEUMA: "One of the beast's heads looked as though it had been dealt a fatal blow, yet the death-wound had healed"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "appeared as though slain to death," blending EMET's participial form with LOGOS's "as though slain" for clarity, retaining "wound of death" from EMET/LOGOS.

**13:4 -- "wage war" vs. "make war":**
- EMET: "make war with it"
- LOGOS: "wage war with him"
- PNEUMA: "wage war against it"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "wage war against it" -- "wage war" is preferred for English idiom (LOGOS/PNEUMA); "against" is clearer than "with"; "it" for the beast (EMET/PNEUMA).

**13:5 -- "to act" vs. "to exercise its power":**
- EMET: "authority to act for forty-two months"
- LOGOS: "authority to act for forty-two months"
- PNEUMA: "permitted to exercise its power for forty-two months"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS with "authority to act," closer to the Greek poiesai.

**13:6 -- Blasphemy clause structure:**
- EMET: "to blaspheme the name of him and the tabernacle of him, the ones tabernacling in the heaven"
- LOGOS: "to blaspheme his name and his tabernacle, those who dwell in heaven"
- PNEUMA: "slandering his name, his dwelling place, and all those who have their home in heaven"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's phrasing, preserving the appositional structure while remaining readable.

**13:7 -- "holy ones" vs. "saints" vs. "God's holy people":**
- EMET: "the holy ones"
- LOGOS: "the saints"
- PNEUMA: "God's holy people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "the saints" (LOGOS), the standard English rendering of hoi hagioi.

**13:7 -- "conquer" vs. "overcome" vs. "overpower":**
- EMET: "to conquer them"
- LOGOS: "to overcome them"
- PNEUMA: "to overpower them"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "to overcome them" (LOGOS).

**13:8 -- "scroll of life" vs. "book of life":**
- EMET: "the scroll of life of the Lamb"
- LOGOS: "the book of life of the Lamb"
- PNEUMA: "the Book of Life belonging to the Lamb"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "the book of life" (LOGOS), the established English phrase.

**13:8 -- "slain" vs. "slaughtered":**
- EMET: "having been slain"
- LOGOS: "who has been slain"
- PNEUMA: "who was slaughtered"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "who was slain" (EMET/LOGOS base vocabulary, LOGOS/PNEUMA clause structure).

**13:9 -- Phrasing of the hearing call:**
- EMET: "If anyone has an ear, let him hear."
- LOGOS: "If anyone has an ear, let him hear."
- PNEUMA: "Whoever has ears must listen carefully:"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS exactly. PNEUMA's paraphrase adds interpretive force not in the Greek.

**13:10 -- Captivity saying:**
- EMET: "If anyone leads into captivity, into captivity he goes."
- LOGOS: "If anyone is destined for captivity, into captivity he goes."
- PNEUMA: "If anyone is bound for captivity, into captivity they will go."
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Destined for" captures the compressed Sinaiticus text better than EMET's literal "[leads] into." PNEUMA's plural "they" shifts from the Greek singular.

**13:10 -- "endurance and faith of the saints":**
- EMET: "the endurance and the faith of the holy ones"
- LOGOS: "the endurance and the faith of the saints"
- PNEUMA: "the call for God's people to stand firm in endurance and faith"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's expansion is interpretive paraphrase.

**13:11 -- "coming up" vs. "rising" vs. "climbing up":**
- EMET: "coming up out of the earth"
- LOGOS: "rising out of the earth"
- PNEUMA: "climbing up out of the earth"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "rising out of the earth" (LOGOS).

**13:11 -- "was speaking as a dragon" vs. "spoke like a dragon":**
- EMET: "it was speaking as a dragon"
- LOGOS: "he spoke like a dragon"
- PNEUMA: "when it spoke, it sounded like a dragon"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "it spoke like a dragon."

**13:12 -- "before it" vs. "in his presence" vs. "on its behalf":**
- EMET: "before it"
- LOGOS: "in his presence"
- PNEUMA: "on its behalf"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "in its presence" (LOGOS's phrasing, neuter pronoun). The Greek enopion means "before/in the presence of," not "on behalf of."

**13:13 -- Fire from heaven:**
- EMET: "fire it makes to come down out of the heaven to the earth before the people"
- LOGOS: "he even makes fire come down from heaven to the earth in the presence of men"
- PNEUMA: "even making fire fall from the sky to the earth while everyone watched"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Heaven" retained over PNEUMA's "sky." "In the presence of men" preserves enopion ton anthropon.

**13:14 -- "telling" vs. "saying" vs. "persuading":**
- EMET: "saying to the ones dwelling upon the earth to make an image"
- LOGOS: "telling those who dwell upon the earth to make an image"
- PNEUMA: "persuading them to construct an image in honor of the beast"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "telling." PNEUMA's "persuading" adds interpretive layers.

**13:14 -- "lived" vs. "came back to life":**
- EMET: "who has the wound of the sword and lived"
- LOGOS: "who has the wound of the sword and yet lived"
- PNEUMA: "that had survived the sword-wound and come back to life"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "yet lived." PNEUMA's "come back to life" is interpretive.

**13:15 -- "spirit" vs. "breath" vs. "life":**
- EMET: "to give spirit to the image"
- LOGOS: "to give breath to the image"
- PNEUMA: "to breathe life into the image"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "breath" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "breathe life into" is a dynamic expansion.

**13:16 -- "mark" vs. "brand":**
- EMET: "a mark"
- LOGOS: "a mark"
- PNEUMA: "a brand"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "a mark" (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek charagma is traditionally rendered "mark."

**13:18 -- Phrasing of the wisdom call:**
- EMET: "The one having understanding, let him count the number"
- LOGOS: "Let him who has understanding calculate the number"
- PNEUMA: "Let the person who has understanding calculate the beast's number"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Calculate" preferred over "count" for psephisato.

**13:18 -- "six hundred sixty-six" vs. "666":**
- EMET: "six hundred sixty-six"
- LOGOS: "six hundred sixty-six"
- PNEUMA: "666"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA writes the number out (EMET/LOGOS), preserving the written-out Greek form.

---

-- SOPHIA
